coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of blue emissive fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from Annona squamosa fruit extract and their diverse applications in the field of catalysis and bio-imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Dhanush, Chandrasekaran
Aravind, Manikka Kubendran
Ashokkumar, Balasubramaniem
Sethuraman, Mathur Gopalakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) were prepared from the fruits of Annona squamosa by hydrothermal method, further they were characterized and studied for the reduction of Reactive Red-120 (RR-120) dye and bio-imaging. Functional groups, surface functionalities present in FNCDs and as well as nitrogen doping were studied using FT-IR and XPS techniques. The optical properties of FNCDs were assessed using UV–Visible and PL spectroscopy. The quantum yield of FNCDs was 14.16%. HR-TEM results revealed the spherical nature of FNCDs with 4 ± 0.25 nm in size and AFM data revealed the height of FNCDs. The amorphous nature of FNCDs was confirmed from the results of XRD, Raman spectra and SAED patterns. TGA data brought out the thermal behavior of FNCDs. These FNCDs were tested for their catalytic role in the reduction of RR-120 dye by NaBH4. Results showed that FNCDs could catalyze the reduction of RR-120 dye within 12 min and the reduction products were analyzed using mass spectral data. The reduction reaction was explained on the basis of the Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism. The possible use of FNCDs as fluorescent probes in cell imaging was also explored by MTT assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Annona squamosa fruit
Fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (FNCDs)
Hydrothermal method
RR-120 dye degradation
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Catalytic competence of FNCDs in the reduction of RR-120 dye
Cytotoxicity and Bio-imaging applications of FNCDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Department of Science and Technology- Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB) for the financial aids provided through the research project entitled “Eco-friendly……… Catalysis” (File No: EMR/2016/006024).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra of the fruit extract and FNCDs.
(a) PL emission spectra of the FNCDs (280–320 nm). (b) PL emission spectra of the FNCDs (330–440 nm). (c) Excitation spectrum of FNCDs.
(a) High Resolution-TEM image. (b) Histogram chart (size arrangement), (c) SAED pattern and (d) AFM image of FNCDs.
FT-IR spectrum of Annona squamosa extract and FNCDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of the FNCDs (survey scan). High resolution XPS spectrum of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s (Deconvoluted).
Reduction absorption spectra of RR-120 dye (a) In the absence of FNCDs. (b) In the presence of FNCDs.
Kinetics plot of RR-120 dye reduction in the presence of FNCDs.
Reduction scheme of RR-120 dye in the presence of FNCDs.
Cytotoxicity plot for HeLa cells with FNCDs.
Fluorescence microscopic images of HeLa cells (incubated with FNCDs) in (a) Bright, (b) 330-380 nm, (c) 420-495 nm and (d) 510-560 nm excitation wavelengths.
Reported works on cell imaging studies with some of the green sources.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of blue emissive fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from Annona squamosa fruit extract and their diverse applications in the field of catalysis and bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute- Deemed to be University, Gandhigram-624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute- Deemed to be University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute- Deemed to be University, Gandhigram-624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Genetic Engineering, School of Biotechnology, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai 625 021, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Genetic Engineering, School of Biotechnology, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai 625 021, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) were prepared from the fruits of Annona squamosa by hydrothermal method, further they were characterized and studied for the reduction of Reactive Red-120 (RR-120) dye and bio-imaging. Functional groups, surface functionalities present in FNCDs and as well as nitrogen doping were studied using FT-IR and XPS techniques. The optical properties of FNCDs were assessed using UV–Visible and PL spectroscopy. The quantum yield of FNCDs was 14.16%. HR-TEM results revealed the spherical nature of FNCDs with 4 ± 0.25 nm in size and AFM data revealed the height of FNCDs. The amorphous nature of FNCDs was confirmed from the results of XRD, Raman spectra and SAED patterns. TGA data brought out the thermal behavior of FNCDs. These FNCDs were tested for their catalytic role in the reduction of RR-120 dye by NaBH4. Results showed that FNCDs could catalyze the reduction of RR-120 dye within 12 min and the reduction products were analyzed using mass spectral data. The reduction reaction was explained on the basis of the Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism. The possible use of FNCDs as fluorescent probes in cell imaging was also explored by MTT assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) receive great attention among researchers nowadays due to their unique properties and size (1–10 nm) [1,2]. The most fascinating properties of FCDs are instant water solubility, chemical inertness, outstanding photoluminescence, least toxicity, good bio-compatibility, good stability, easy functionalization and low-cost synthesis [3]. FCDs have been demonstrated to play a vital role in influencing catalytic reactions (photo & electro) [4,5], sensing, bio-imaging, oxygen reduction reactions, oxygen and hydrogen evolution reactions, solar cells, food safety applications and electrode component material for batteries and supercapacitors [6–9]. There are many methods reported already for the preparation of FCDs, which include acidic oxidation, chemical oxidation, electrochemical synthesis, ultrasonic/microwave method, hydrothermal carbonization and microwave synthesis [10,11]. To enhance the physical, chemical and optical properties of the FCDs, doping is done. On doping with several hetero atoms, metals and various functional moieties, the electron-donating accepting nature of the FCDs are enhanced which in turn enhances the strong fluorescence emission capability justifying their application in the field of photocatalysis [12]and bio-imaging [13]. In recent days, FCDs derived from natural sources like fruits, vegetables, plants, juices, beverages and human derivatives gain more attention among researchers. Phyto- constituents present in the precursors act as the source of carbon for the synthesis of FCDs [14].

Based on the above criteria, Annona squamosa (Custard apple) is a fruit from a small tree or a semi-shrub belonging to the Annonaceae family. Flavonoids, phenols, saponins, tannins, alkaloids and phytosterols are the phyto-constituents present in Annona squamosa fruit and they play a major role as the carbon precursor for the synthesis of FNCDs. This plant exhibits many medicinal activities like anti-inflammatory, anti-fungal, anti-cancer and anti- oxidant effects [15]. The fruit is rich in vitamin B1, calcium, phosphorous and iron. This species is abundantly distributed in the sub-tropical and tropical regions of Asia, America and Africa. It is also cultivated in Chile, Peru, Bolivia, Mexico, Spain and Ecuador. Spain is the world’s largest producer of custard apples [16].

FCDs were also used as a catalyst in the dye degradation of water contaminants. FCDs are potential catalysts because they possess an electron mediator nature (i.e. electron donor and acceptor nature) [17]. It is very well known that dyes and effluents cause health issues to various living organisms including human beings. Reactive Red 120 (RR-120) is a dye used extensively which causes health hazards in both human beings and animals. Aromatic rings present in the RR-120 dye structure make it resistant towards natural bio-degradation. Untreated dye effluents containing RR-120 dye when let into the water bodies cause severe damage to the aquatic lives causing skin irritation, dermatitis and allergy. These dye stuffs also provoke skin cancer and finally lead to mutation in humans. Various physicochemical and biological methods had been followed for the treatment of effluents containing this dye. Among the methods, degradation serves as a common and rapid deactivation technique for the removal of textile dyes [18].

In the field of medicine, bio-imaging applications play a vital role in cancer diagnosis and treatment. Initially, metals-based semi-conductor quantum dots (SQDs) and conventional organic dyes were used as staining probes due to their fluorescent nature. Later, owing to uncertain fluorescence and heavy metal toxicity, their utility in the field of medicine faded slowly [19,20]. Typical advantages of FCDs like multicolor emission profile, prominent bio-compatibility, small sizes, excellent photo-stability and low toxicity enabled them as an excellent staining agent in the field of fluorescence bio-imaging [21,22]. In biomedical research, HeLa cells are the oldest and most commonly utilized uterine cervical carcinoma cell line. Due to their fertile and durable nature, HeLa cells are normally used for the development of cancer research, vaccines and confocal fluorescence imaging [23]. There are reports available on the use of FCDs as theranostic agents [24,25].

In this research work, fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) have been prepared from the fruit of Annona squamosa by hydrothermal method. The fruit of Annona squamosa was used as the carbon-rich source with aq·NH3 as the source of nitrogen. Characterization of FNCDs was done using surface morphological, optical and structural analysis. The catalytic efficiency of FNCDs was evaluated in the reduction of RR-120 dye as an effective alternative to the existing dye removal methods. Further, the role of FNCDs as staining agents is also proved to exploit them as theranostic agents.

UV–Visible spectroscopy is a helpful tool for characterizing the optical properties of FNCDs. Fig. 1 displays the UV–Visible absorption spectra of the clear fruit extract and FNCDs. Fruit extract showed a couple of distinct absorption maxima at 282 & 330 nm. In FNCDs, a shoulder at 272 nm could be attributed to the π- π* transition of the C = C moieties and an another shoulder at 306 nm was due to C = O/C = N moieties (n- π*) transitions [6]. There was a definite variation in the absorption maxima of Annona squamosa fruit extract upon the addition of aqueous ammonia. This was due to the transformation of flavonoids and polyphenols present in the fruit extract to ammonium phenolate ions [26].

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy is antithetical to UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. FNCDs at 400 nm exhibited a high bright blue emission fluorescence when excited at 320 nm excitation wavelength and this was because of the π*- π transitions ( Fig. 2 a). Later, when there was an increase in the excitation wavelength from 330 to 440 nm there was a decrease in the fluorescence emission which was due to the n- π* electronic transitions ( Fig. 2b). FNCDs displayed 320 nm excitation at 400 nm emission wavelength which showed the excitation-dependent nature [27] ( Fig. 2c). This might also be due to their variation in size, surface states, surface defects and functional moieties. Doping with nitrogen enhanced the structural defects, altered exterior surface functional moieties and influenced the interactions of the carbon atoms with the neighboring moieties [28]. Nitrogen doping produced new surface state energy levels and enhances radiative recombination. The extent of conjugation was also enhanced with the electron-donating N dopant by rising the e- transitions from the ground state to the lowest excited singlet state [29]. Due to their diverse color emitting properties, FNCDs could be utilized in the area of bio-imaging and fluorescent biomedical applications [30]. The quantum yield of FNCDs was about 14.16% [31].

High Resolution-TEM analysis showed the size and morphological characteristics of FNCDs ( Fig. 3 a-c). Dilute FNCDs placed on the copper grids showed a size range of 4 ± 0.25 nm without any apparent aggregation. The dots were spherical in shape and their average size distribution ranged from 2 to 7 nm. Diffused SAED patterns revealed the amorphous nature of the FNCDs and showed the presence of sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon [32]. Fig. 3d displayed the monodispersed AFM image of FNCDs with the corresponding height profile graph. The height of FNCDs was 4.5 nm in size which are slightly higher than the HR-TEM data because of the relative humidity in the AFM measurement [33,34]. Thus, AFM data matched well with HR-TEM data and confirmed the spherical nature of FNCDs.

FESEM analysis was also done to explore the surface morphological characteristics of FNCDs. Fig. S.1. displayed the various FESEM images of FNCDs. FNCDs were in cluster based framework and they possess high density growth which led to some agglomerations in the solid phase [35,36]. The energy dispersive spectrum established the percentage of C (76.52%), O (14.35%) and N (9.13%) in FNCDs (Fig. 4 ).

Fig. 5 displayed the FT-IR spectrum of Annona squamosa extract and the FNCDs. FT-IR spectrum of Annona squamosa extract showed various bands at 3427 cm−1 (–OH), 2928 cm−1 (–CH), 1734 cm−1 (-C = O), 1634 cm−1 (-C = C-), 1381 cm−1 (–COO), 1108 cm−1 (-C-O-C-) and 668 cm−1 (C–H) showing the presence of various moieties. In FNCDs, bands appeared at 3413 cm−1 (–OH/NH), 2938 cm−1 (–CH), 1595 cm−1 (-C = O/-C = C-), 1392 cm−1 (–COO), 1116 cm−1 (-C-O-C-/-C-N), 1034 cm−1 (C-O), 801 cm−1 (–OH/NH2) and 660 cm−1 (C–H) correspondingly. Broad stretched band around 3450 cm−1 to 3300 cm−1 was because of the overlay of –OH/–NH functional moieties or because of hetero atom doping. Thus, the FT-IR spectrum of FNCDs showed the doping of N and also the presence of amine, acid, hydroxyl and carbonyl moieties present over the surface of FNCDs [37,38].

The XRD pattern of FNCDs is shown in Fig. 6
. This analysis was followed to enlighten the nature, structure and phase of FNCDs. A strong peak around 28.510 and a weak peak around 40.710 representing 2ɵ values correlate to the presence of the (002) and (100) planes. Peaks around 28.510 showed that FNCDs were poorly crystalline in nature. It also indicates the presence of amorphous carbon present in FNCDs which were arranged randomly [39]. The 2ɵ values at 0.301 nm and 0.212 nm were the d-spacing values of (002) and (001) planes respectively which were less than the d-spacing value of graphite [40]. Thus, the aforesaid explanations confirm that FNCDs were amorphous with irregular geometry.

XPS data revealed the elemental states, surface chemical composition, surface distribution and functionalization of the elements present. The survey scan spectrum of FNCDs ( Fig. 9 a) exhibited three different peaks at 281.6 eV, 396 eV and 528 eV showing the presence of C1s (78.34%), N1s (8.97%) and O1s (12.69%) elemental states respectively. Fig. 9b showed three deconvoluted peaks of C1s at 284.6 eV, 285.8 eV & 292.6 eV indicating the presence of C–C/C = C (sp2 graphite), C-O/C-N and π- π* transitions [38,46]. Fig. 9c exhibited three deconvoluted peaks of N1s at 398.9 eV, 400 eV and 400.6 eV showing the existence of C-NH2/N–H/C-N-C, N-(C)3 & pyridinic/pyrrolic type N atoms [46–48]. Fig. 9d indicated three different deconvoluted peaks of O1s at 530.6 eV, 531.7 eV and 533.1 eV showing the existence of C = O, C-OH and C-O-C groups [49,50]. Finally, these results supported the outcomes of the energy dispersive spectrum, FT-IR spectrum and affirm the doping of nitrogen on FNCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic competence of FNCDs in the reduction of RR-120 dye
ce:section.ce:para:
One of the most fascinating properties of CDs is that they act as an excellent catalytic agents. To assess their competence, catalytic reduction of RR-120 dye was performed with NaBH4 using FNCDs (catalyst). RR dye in an aqueous medium displayed its characteristic absorption of around 500 nm [51]. Using NaBH4 a series of spectroscopic runs were performed in the presence and absence of FNCDs (catalyst) ( Fig. 10
a & b). UV–Visible spectrophotometer was used to measure the spectral changes at definite time intervals. In the absence of FNCDs, the spectroscopic measurements of RR-120 showed only 40% of reduction after 50 min. Further, an increase of time did not produce any appreciable reduction of the RR-120 dye. But when the same reaction was performed in the presence of FNCDs (20 µl), there was a complete reduction of the RR-120 dye in 12 min which showed the catalytic competence of the FNCDs. During reduction, the azo bonds break first (-N = N-) leading to the change in the chromophoric group of RR-120 dye and finally leading to complete reduction and decolorization of the dye[52]. When the degradation was complete, the UV spectrum had only the shoulders of the respective FNCDs which showed that FNCDs acted as an only catalyst and did not interfere in the chemical reaction during the reduction.

Langmuir- Hinshelwood mechanism was taken as a basis to explain the reduction mechanism of RR-120 dye. FNCDs provide the required surface for the adsorption of BH4 - ions and RR-120 dye enabling the transmission of electrons from BH4 - to RR-120 dye. Donation of lone pair of electrons to the surface of FNCDs could facilitate the efficient transmission of electrons. So, the energy border between the reactant and product was diminished and consequently, the reaction was accelerated. Finally, the reaction was completely terminated with the evolution of hydrogen gas [53]. The percentage of dye reduction was explained from the equation,
%
of
reduction =
R
0
-
R
t
/
R
0
x
100
where, R0 - initial abs.; Rt – abs. @ time (t).

Pseudo first-order kinetics equation [ln (Rt/R0) = -kt] was used to evaluate the rate constant (k) for the reduction of RR-120 dye. Fig. 11
. showed the kinetics plot between time (minutes) and (Rt/R0) in their corresponding X and Y axes. The graph showed a good linearity where R2, k and the slope values were obtained from the linear plot. The respective R2, k and slope values are 0.97187, 0.44724 min−1 and −0.3940. But, in the reduction of RR-120 dye without FNCDs a good linear plot was not obtained. Thus, this work showed the ability of FNCDs as a catalyst in the reduction of RR-120 dye. The reduction scheme of RR-120 dye is displayed in Fig. 12
. Mass spectral data of the dye solution confirmed the products formed after reduction (Fig. S.2). Fragments corresponding to m/z values 1085.3095 and 195.8829 were identified and the reduction reaction shown in Fig. 12 was confirmed [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and Bio-imaging applications of FNCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
FNCDs possess unique fluorescent and fascinating properties which enable them to act as suitable staining probes for biological and biomedical applications. In the MTT assay, cells without FNCDs treatment served as a control. There was about 100% viability when the HeLa cells were treated with 10 µg/ml of FNCDs. On increasing the concentration of FNCDs, there was a mild decrease in the viability of cells. More than 80% of cell viability was observed in HeLa cells at 50 µg/ml concentration of FNCDs. Thus, from the MTT assay it could be observed that cell viability was not afflicted much even at high concentrations of FNCDs and the cytotoxicity plot is shown in Fig. 13
. It also showed that FNCDs were less toxic and bio-compatible. It was clear from these studies that FNCDs could be used for cellular imaging of HeLa cells [55–57].

Fig. 14. displayed the fluorescent microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with the synthesized FNCDs. FNCDs seem to possess a broad range of fluorescence spectra from 300 to 600 nm showing that they act as promising candidates for bio-imaging and bio-labeling [41]. Fig. 14
(a-d) showed the images of the HeLa cells at different excitation wavelengths. The images indicated the cellular uptake of the FNCDs and the microscope efficiently captured the incubated cells in different fields correspondingly. Thus, the images revealed the penetration and homogeneous diffusion of FNCDs across the cell membranes of the HeLa cells. The imaging was captured in 20 X magnification. These observations show that the FNCDs from Annona squamosa extract could be beneficially utilized as a capable alternate staining agent for bio-imaging and other biological fluorescence applications [58]. Table. 1 shows some of the green sources utilized for HeLa cell imaging applications.

This paper reports a facile and economical hydrothermal process for the preparation of FNCDs from Annona squamosa fruit extract. The synthesized FNCDs were spherical in shape and possess its own optical properties. FT-IR and XPS data proved the doping of nitrogen onto FNCDs. Amorphous nature and surface properties were interpreted from the XRD, Raman and SAED data. Moreover, FNCDs were found to function as an efficient catalyst in the reduction of RR-120 dye. In the presence of FNCDs, it took 12 min for the complete reduction of RR-120 dye. But in the absence of FNCDs, only 40% of the reduction was observed even after 50 min. FNCDs exhibited very low toxicity even at high concentrations with HeLa cells. There was only about 20% of the decrease in the cell viability even at 50 µg/ml of FNCDs. Thus, owing to the less toxicity and good bio-compatibility the biogenic FNCDs can be effectively utilized as a staining probe for biological applications.

Chandrasekaran Dhanush: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Manikka Kubendran Aravind: Formal analysis, Data curation. Balasubramaniem Ashokkumar: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Mathur Gopalakrishnan Sethuraman: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Department of Science and Technology- Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB) for the financial aids provided through the research project entitled “Eco-friendly……… Catalysis” (File No: EMR/2016/006024).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114097.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
